name: Windows 1.01
facts: Microsoft first demonstrated a window manager to the press in September 1983.

By supporting cooperative multitasking in tiled windows when using well-behaved applications that only used DOS system calls and permitting non-well-behaved applications to run in a full screen, Windows differed from both Visi On and Apple Computer's Lisa by immediately offering many applications.

Unlike Visi On, Windows developers did not need to use Unix to develop IBM PC applications; Microsoft planned to encourage other companies, including competitors, to develop programs for Windows by not requiring a Microsoft user interface in their applications.
On November 20, 1985 (40 years ago), the first retail release, Windows 1.01, was released in the United States at a cost of US$99 (equivalent to about $296.00 in 2025).

name: Windows 2.01
facts: The Windows/386 release is labeled 2.01 and is dated September 1987
The Windows/386 2.01 variant was finalized and released as an OEM version for the Compaq DeskPro 386 in September 1987.
This version was finalized early to meet contractual obligations to bundle it with new 386 computers.
The more widely known Windows 2.03 was released shortly after on December 9, 1987.
It allowed multiple DOS applications to run in parallel using virtual 8086 mode, a significant improvement over the base Windows 2.0.
While 'Windows 2.01' is technically a version number, this specific release was labeled and marketed as Windows/386, differentiating it from the standard Windows 2.03 that ran in Real Mode.
In summary, Windows/386 2.01 is a version of Windows 2.0, but specifically the '386' variant, not the standard, generic Windows 2.01 release.

name: Windows 2.03
facts: The product includes two different variants: a base edition for 8086 real mode, and Windows/386, an enhanced edition for i386 protected mode.
Unlike its predecessor, Windows 2.0 allows the user to overlap and resize application windows.
Support for 16-color VGA graphics, EMS memory, and new capabilities of the i386 CPU in some versions were also added.
Windows 2.0 is the last version of Windows that ran solely on floppy disks.
Windows 2.0 was dependent on the DOS system and random-access memory was restricted to a maximum of 1 MB due to running in real mode.

facts: This os another version, Windows for Workgroups 3.11, was released on November 8, 1993

The release received network improvements; a Winsock package was released for Windows for Workgroups, although it was later replaced by a 32-bit stack add-on package (codenamed Wolverine) that provided TCP/IP support in Windows for Workgroups 3.11
Its connectivity with NetWare networks was increased, while it also introduced support for Open Data-Link Interface (ODI) cards and Internetwork Packet Exchange (IPX) drivers

name: Windows 8
facts: Windows 8 added support for USB 3.0, near-field communication, and cloud computing, as well as a new lock screen with clock and notifications.
New features and functionality include hard disk 4KB Advanced Format support
Windows 8 can natively mount ISOs, and Task Manager is much improved - and, perhaps most importantly, Windows 8 now has a built-in, continuous backup tool
It was the first Windows version to support ARM architecture under the Windows RT branding
CPUs without PAE, SSE2 and NX are unsupported in this version
Additional security features—including built-in antivirus software, integration with Microsoft SmartScreen phishing filtering, and support for Secure Boot on supported devices—were introduced

Windows 8 introduced major changes to the operating system's platform and user interface with the intention to improve its user experience on tablets, where Windows competed with mobile operating systems such as Android and iOS
Windows 8 introduces a new style of applications, Windows Store apps

requirements.name: Minimum
cpu: 1 GHz clock rate IA-32 or x64 architecture with at least 2 cores
Support for PAE, NX and SSE2
vga: DirectX 9 graphics device WDDM 1.0 or higher driver

ports.mouse: Keyboard and mouse

cpu: x64 architecture Second Level Address Translation (SLAT) support for Hyper-V

other: UEFI v2.3.1 Errata B with Microsoft Windows Certification Authority in its database
Trusted Platform Module (TPM) Internet connectivity

name: Windows 8.1
facts: The 64-bit variants of Windows 8.1 no longer support processors which do not implement the double-width compare and exchange (CMPXCHG16B) CPU instruction (which the installer reports as a lack of support for 'CompareExchange128').
On compatible hardware, Windows 8.1 also features a transparent 'device encryption' system based on BitLocker.
Windows Defender includes an intrusion detection system which can scan network activity for signs of malware.
Windows 8.1 adds support for 3D printing, pairing with printers using NFC tags, Wi-Fi Direct, Miracast media streaming, tethering,and NVMe.
Windows 8.1 received more positive reviews than Windows 8.
Microsoft markets Windows 8.1 as an 'update' for Windows 8, avoiding the term 'upgrade'.

On May 23, 2014, Microsoft officially announced Windows 8.1 with Bing, a new, lower-cost version of its operating system designed specifically for original equipment manufacturers (OEMs) to preinstall on budget-friendly laptops, tablets, and desktop computers.

name: Windows 10
facts: Windows 10 received generally positive reviews.
It is the last version of Windows that supports 32-bit processors, BIOS firmware, and systems with no TPM or TPM 1.2.
This is the latest version, which officially does not check the processor model before installation.

Windows 10 is distributed digitally through the 'Media Creation Tool', which is functionally identical to the Windows 8 online installer, and can also be used to generate an ISO image or USB install media.
The basic hardware requirements to install Windows 10 were initially the same as those for Windows 8 and Windows 8.1, and only slightly higher than for Windows 7 and Windows Vista.
Mainstream builds of Windows 10 are labeled 'YYMM', with YY representing the two-digit year and MM representing the month of planned release.
Starting with version 20H2, Windows 10 release nomenclature changed from the year and month pattern to a year and half-year pattern (YYH1, YYH2).
The update has reached end of service on May 9, 2017 in the Current Branch and Current Branch for Business.
The Enterprise LTSB edition has reached end of service on October 14, 2025.

name: Windows 10 version 1607
facts: The third stable build of Windows 10 is called version 1607, known as the Anniversary Update.
This version is the basis for 'LTSB 2016', the first upgrade to the LTSB since Windows 10's release.
The first LTSB release, based on the original release of Windows 10 (version 1507), has been retroactively named 'LTSB 2015'.
The update has reached end of service on April 10, 2018 in the Semi-Annual Channel.
he Enterprise, IoT Enterprise and Education editions have reached end of service on April 9, 2019.
Support of this update for systems based on Intel Clover Trail chipset was available until January 10, 2023.
The Enterprise LTSB and IoT Enterprise LTSB editions will reach end of service on October 13, 2026.

name: Windows 10 version 1703
facts: The version was announced on October 26, 2016.
The version was released for manual installation via Windows 10 Upgrade Assistant and Media Creation Tool tools on April 5, 2017.
The version was released for general availability on April 11, 2017.

Windows 10 is distributed digitally through the 'Media Creation Tool', which is functionally identical to the Windows 8 online installer, and can also be used to generate an ISO image or USB install media.
Windows 10 versions 1703 and later do not support Intel Clover Trail system-on-chips, per Microsoft's stated policy of only providing updates for devices during their OEM support period.

requirements.name: Minimum
cpu: 1 GHz or faster processor or System on a Chip (SoC)
It must support PAE, NX, and SSE2, along with CMPXCHG16b, LAHF/SAHF, and PrefetchW for 64-bit
vga: DirectX 9 graphics device WDDM 1.0 or higher driver

purposes: USB Ports (Standard 2.0 or higher)
They are required to install Windows 10 from a USB drive, since most modern computers do not have CD/DVD drives
At a minimum, you need to connect a bootable USB drive for the installation
This is required to connect a keyboard and mouse during setup
Necessary to display old-format analog video on the screen
Necessary to display new-format digital video on the screen
Required to create a Microsoft account, download updates, and activate the operating system

requirements.name: Recommended
cpu: Intel Core: 7th Gen (e.g., i7-7700, i5-7600), 6th Gen (e.g., i7-6700, i5-6600), and 5th Gen (e.g., i7-5700HQ)
AMD: A-Series Ax-9xxx, E-Series Ex-9xxx, FX-9xxx, and early Ryzen 3/5/7 2xxx series

media: Any optical disc drive capable of reading DVD-ROM media.
ports.mouse: Keyboard, mouse and multi-touch display
other: UEFI v2.3.1 Errata B with Microsoft Windows Certification Authority in its database
Trusted Platform Module (TPM) Internet connectivity

name: Windows 10 version 1709
facts: The update has reached end of service on April 9, 2019 for Home, Pro, Pro Education, Pro for Workstations, and IoT Core editions.
The Enterprise and Education editions would have originally reached end of service on April 14, 2020, but this was postponed to October 13 of the same year due to the COVID-19 pandemic.

requirements.name: Minimum
cpu: 1 GHz clock rate IA-32 or x86-64 architecture with at least 2 cores
1.4 GHz minimum is often cited for 64-bit editions to ensure stable performance.
vga: DirectX 9 graphics device WDDM 1.0 or higher driver

purposes: USB Ports (Standard 2.0 or higher)
They are required to install Windows 10 from a USB drive, since most modern computers do not have CD/DVD drives
At a minimum, you need to connect a bootable USB drive for the installation
This is required to connect a keyboard and mouse during setup

Necessary to display new-format digital video on the screen
Required to create a Microsoft account, download updates, and activate the operating system

Ryzen 3, 5, or 7 processors, particularly A-Series (A10, A12, A6) and early Athlon/EPYC chips supported in 2017
Budget/Mobile: Intel Celeron 3000/4000 series (e.g., J4005, N4000) or Intel Core m3-6Y30/m5-6Y54.

media: Any optical disc drive capable of reading DVD-ROM media.
ports.mouse: Keyboard, mouse and multi-touch display

Trusted Platform Module (TPM) Internet connectivity

name: Windows 10 version 1803
facts: The update has reached end of service on November 12, 2019 for Home, Pro, Pro for Workstations and IoT Core editions.

The Enterprise, IoT Enterprise and Education editions would have originally reached end of service on November 10, 2020, but this was postponed to May 11, 2021 due to the 'global health crisis', in reference to the COVID-19 pandemic.

name: Windows 10 version 1809
facts: The update has reached end of service on November 10, 2020 for Home, Pro, Pro Education, Pro for Workstations and IoT Core editions.
The Enterprise, IoT Enterprise and Education editions have reached end of service on May 11, 2021.
The Enterprise LTSC, IoT Enterprise LTSC and IoT Core LTSC editions will reach end of service on January 9, 2029.

Windows 10 Long-Term Servicing Branch (LTSB) was rebranded to Long-Term Servicing Channel (LTSC) in late 2018, specifically with the release of the Windows 10 Enterprise LTSC 2019 version (version 1809).

name: Windows 10 version 20H2

The update has reached end of service on May 10, 2022 for Home, Pro, Pro Education, Pro for Workstations and Team (for Surface Hub devices) editions.
The Enterprise, Enterprise multi-session, IoT Enterprise and Education editions have reached end of service on May 9, 2023.

name: Windows 10 version 21H2
facts: The thirteenth stable build of Windows 10, version 21H2, was released to the public on November 16, 2021, after being on the Beta Channel since July 15, 2021.
Added GPU compute support in the Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL) and Azure IoT Edge for Linux on Windows (EFLOW) deployments.
Added support for NVMe 2.0.
The update has reached end of service on June 13, 2023 for Home, Pro, Pro Education and Pro for Workstations editions.
The Enterprise, Enterprise multi-session, IoT Enterprise and Education editions have reached end of service on June 11, 2024.
The Enterprise LTSC and IoT Enterprise LTSC editions will reach end of service on January 12, 2027 and January 13, 2032, respectively.

name: Windows 10 version 22H2
facts: It is the last version of Windows that supports 32-bit processors, BIOS firmware, and systems with no TPM or TPM 1.2.
This is the latest version, which officially does not check the processor model before installation.

Windows 10 is distributed digitally through the 'Media Creation Tool', which is functionally identical to the Windows 8 online installer, and can also be used to generate an ISO image or USB install media.
The basic hardware requirements to install Windows 10 were initially the same as those for Windows 8 and Windows 8.1, and only slightly higher than for Windows 7 and Windows Vista.

requirements.name: Minimum
cpu: 1 GHz clock rate IA-32 or x86-64 architecture with at least 2 cores
Support for PAE, NX and SSE2
x86-64 CPUs must also support CMPXCHG16B, PrefetchW, and LAHF/SAHF instructions
media: Any optical disc drive capable of reading DVD-ROM media.
vga: DirectX 9 graphics device WDDM 1.0 or higher driver
